element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player explores at own pace, discovers library and linking books; no threats or time limits

element_introduction_order.element: Red and Blue linking books (plot devices)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Books reveal they are traps containing Sirrus and Achenar; player must understand their nature to progress
Self-contained puzzle world requiring logical problem-solving; player gains familiarity with Age mechanics

element_introduction_order.first_appears: After initial Myst puzzles (ship raising puzzle)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Second Age introducing varied environmental puzzle types
Final Age requiring integration of previous puzzle-solving knowledge
Single-page carry limit encourages deliberate information gathering; pages reveal character motivations
Player learns interaction model through non-threatening experimentation

typical_relief_triggers: Puzzle solved (unlocks new area or Age)
Returning to Myst Island hub (safe space)
Successful navigation via Zip feature (reduced friction)
typical_tension_triggers: Encountering new Age with unclear objectives
Discovering trap nature of red/blue books
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Player-paced exploration with puzzle gates; no time pressure or fail states create contemplative rhythm; tension builds through accumulating mystery rather than mechanical threat

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Zip feature appearance (lightning bolt cursor) marks already-explored paths; player can choose rapid transit or leisurely exploration
spatial_grammar.secret_convention: Journal pages hidden in specific locations reward thorough exploration; backstory pages dispersed across Ages encourage complete world traversal

difficulty_curve.early_game: Gentle introduction on Myst Island; teach interaction model via non-threatening object manipulation and navigation; primary puzzle (ship raising) is introductory-level
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Difficulty escalates through Selenitic and Stoneship Ages; puzzles become logically interrelated; player must synthesize observations across environments
difficulty_curve.late_game: Mechanical and Channelwood Ages present complex puzzle chains requiring retention of earlier clues and problem-solving strategies
notable_spikes: First exposure to trap book mechanics (conceptual complexity)
Mechanical Age interrelated puzzle systems (systemic complexity)

notable_level_patterns.description: Each Age is a self-contained mini-world with discrete puzzle challenges that must be solved to fully explore and unlock progression. Ages share similar progression structure but vary thematically (mechanical, nautical, sound-based, botanical).
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Stoneship Age (requires ship raising puzzle on Myst Island to access)

notable_level_patterns.description: Myst Island functions as a central hub. Library contains linking books to four distinct Ages. Player must return to Myst Island to access different Ages, creating natural pacing breaks.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Myst Island library as central nexus

notable_level_patterns.description: Critical clues are distributed across multiple locations and Ages. Player must synthesize information from journal pages, environmental clues, and puzzle results to understand narrative and solve final puzzle.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Red and blue book trap mechanics require observations from multiple Ages to understand

notable_level_patterns.description: No mandatory quest order, but puzzle requirements create implicit progression. Player can explore Ages in various orders once unlocked, but solution paths eventually converge.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Player can explore Selenitic or Stoneship first, but must solve puzzles in both to progress

sources_used: Wikipedia - Myst article (Gameplay and Plot sections)
